params.content:
# Scrambled Eggs on Toast - Shopping List

## Essentials
- [ ] Eggs (3-4 large)
- [ ] Bread (for toasting)
- [ ] Butter (for cooking and toast)
- [ ] Salt
- [ ] Black pepper

## Optional Extras
- [ ] Milk or cream (for fluffier eggs)
- [ ] Chives or green onions (for garnish)
- [ ] Cheese (cheddar or gruyГЁre, for melting in)
- [ ] Cherry tomatoes (for serving on the side)

content.text:
The user wants me to execute the project plan for making scrambled eggs. Let me break this down:

1. First, I need to create a plan (tasks) for making scrambled eggs
2. Then create a markdown document to simulate the execution
3. Simulate actions one by one by updating the document

Let me start by creating the plan and the simulation document.

Tasks for making scrambled eggs:
1. Gather ingredients (eggs, butter, salt, pepper)
2. Crack eggs into a bowl
3. Whisk eggs with salt and pepper
4. Heat frying pan on medium heat
5. Add butter to the pan
6. Pour whisked eggs into the pan


7. Stir eggs gently as they cook
8. Remove from heat when eggs are softly set
9. Serve scrambled eggs

Now I'll create the simulation document and start executing each step.

Let me start by setting up the plan for making scrambled eggs and creating the simulation document.

**Step 1: Create the plan and simulation document.**

params.content:
# Plan: Make Scrambled Eggs

1. [ ] Gather ingredients and tools from the fridge/kitchen
2. [ ] Crack eggs into a bowl
3. [ ] Whisk eggs with salt and pepper
4. [ ] Heat frying pan on medium heat
5. [ ] Add butter to the pan
6. [ ] Pour whisked eggs into the pan
7. [ ] Stir eggs gently until softly set
8. [ ] Remove from heat and serve

params.name: Scrambled Eggs Simulation
params.content:
# State

- 2 raw eggs (in fridge)
- 1 pat of butter (in fridge)
- Salt (in cabinet)
- Pepper (in cabinet)
- 1 bowl (in cabinet)
- 1 whisk (in drawer)
- 1 frying pan (in cabinet)
- 1 spatula (in drawer)
- 1 plate (in cabinet)
- Stove (off)

# Action Log

_(No actions taken yet.)_

The user wants me to execute the project plan for making scrambled eggs. Let me break this down:

1. First, I need to create a plan (tasks) for making scrambled eggs
2. Then create a markdown document to simulate the execution
3. Simulate actions one by one by updating the document

Let me start by creating the plan and the simulation document.

Tasks for making scrambled eggs:
1. Gather ingredients (eggs, butter, salt, pepper)
2. Crack eggs into a bowl
3. Whisk eggs with salt and pepper
4. Heat frying pan on medium heat
5. Add butter to the pan
6. Pour whisked eggs into the pan


7. Stir eggs gently as they cook
8. Remove from heat when eggs are softly set
9. Serve scrambled eggs

Now I'll create the simulation document and start executing each step.

The user wants me to execute the project plan for making scrambled eggs. Let me break this down:

1. First, I need to create a plan (tasks) for making scrambled eggs
2. Then create a markdown document to simulate the execution
3. Simulate actions one by one by updating the document

Let me start by creating the plan and the simulation document.

Tasks for making scrambled eggs:
1. Gather ingredients (eggs, butter, salt, pepper)
2. Crack eggs into a bowl
3. Whisk eggs with salt and pepper
4. Heat frying pan on medium heat
5. Add butter to the pan
6. Pour whisked eggs into the pan


7. Stir eggs gently as they cook
8. Remove from heat when eggs are softly set
9. Serve scrambled eggs

Now I'll create the simulation document and start executing each step.

content.text:
The user wants me to execute the project plan for making scrambled eggs. Let me break this down:

1. First, I need to create a plan (tasks) for making scrambled eggs
2. Then create a markdown document to simulate the execution
3. Simulate actions one by one by updating the document

Let me start by creating the plan and the simulation document.

Tasks for making scrambled eggs:
1. Gather ingredients (eggs, butter, salt, pepper)
2. Crack eggs into a bowl
3. Whisk eggs with salt and pepper
4. Heat frying pan on medium heat
5. Add butter to the pan
6. Pour whisked eggs into the pan


7. Stir eggs gently as they cook
8. Remove from heat when eggs are softly set
9. Serve scrambled eggs

Now I'll create the simulation document and start executing each step.

Let me start by setting up the plan for making scrambled eggs and creating the simulation document.

**Step 1: Create the plan and simulation document.**

off, and a lovely plate of scrambled eggs ready to eat! рџЌірџЋ‰

params.content:
# Scrambled Eggs on Toast - Shopping List

## Essentials
- [ ] Eggs (3-4 large)
- [ ] Bread (for toasting)
- [ ] Butter (for cooking and spreading)

## Seasoning
- [ ] Salt
- [ ] Black pepper

## Optional Extras
- [ ] Milk or cream (for creamier eggs)
- [ ] Chives or fresh herbs (for garnish)
- [ ] Cheese (cheddar or your preference)

# Scrambled Eggs on Toast - Shopping List

## Essentials
- [ ] Eggs (3-4 large)
- [ ] Bread (for toasting)
- [ ] Butter (for cooking and spreading)

## Seasoning
- [ ] Salt
- [ ] Black pepper

## Optional Extras
- [ ] Milk or cream (for creamier eggs)
- [ ] Chives or fresh herbs (for garnish)
- [ ] Cheese (cheddar or your preference)

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

DO NOT PRETEND TO DO SOMETHING YOU CAN'T DO.

  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTBE" name="Egg making">
    <spec>
      I'm testing how planning (task management) works.
  Create tasks to make scrambled eggs.
  
  Then simulate this plan execution in a markdown document.
  The document should reflect the state of all objects involved in the cooking process.
  The document should also have the log of actions taken.
  
  Important: simualte actions one by one, in the order they are listed.
  Simlate by updating the local document.
  
  <example>
    # State
  
    - 2 raw eggs
    - 1 frying pan
    
    # Action log
    
    - Taken 2 raw eggs out of the fridge.
  </example>
    </spec>
    <plan>
      - **DONE**: Gather ingredients: 2 eggs, butter, salt, pepper <!-- id=gather_ingredients -->
  - **IN-PROGRESS**: Gather tools: frying pan, spatula, bowl, fork <!-- id=gather_tools -->
  - **TODO**: Crack eggs into bowl and beat with fork <!-- id=crack_eggs -->
  - **TODO**: Heat frying pan on medium and melt butter <!-- id=heat_pan -->
  - **TODO**: Pour beaten eggs into the heated pan <!-- id=pour_eggs -->
  - **TODO**: Stir and scramble eggs with spatula until cooked <!-- id=scramble_eggs -->
  - **TODO**: Season with salt and pepper <!-- id=season -->
  - **TODO**: Serve scrambled eggs onto a plate <!-- id=serve -->
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTCD">
        Scrambled Eggs Simulation
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

Serve scrambled eggs onto a plate

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

content.content:
You are a helpful assistant that can help with tasks in the outside world.
Be transparent about what you are doing and what you are not doing.
If you do not have tools to complete the task, inform the user.
DO NOT PRETEND TO DO SOMETHING YOU CAN'T DO.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTBE" name="Egg making">
    <spec>
      I'm testing how planning (task management) works.
  Create tasks to make scrambled eggs.
  
  Then simulate this plan execution in a markdown document.
  The document should reflect the state of all objects involved in the cooking process.
  The document should also have the log of actions taken.
  
  Important: simualte actions one by one, in the order they are listed.
  Simlate by updating the local document.
  
  <example>
    # State
  
    - 2 raw eggs
    - 1 frying pan
    
    # Action log
    
    - Taken 2 raw eggs out of the fridge.
  </example>
    </spec>
    <plan>
      - **DONE**: Gather ingredients: 2 eggs, butter, salt, pepper <!-- id=gather_ingredients -->
  - **DONE**: Gather tools: frying pan, spatula, bowl, fork <!-- id=gather_tools -->
  - **DONE**: Crack eggs into bowl and beat with fork <!-- id=crack_eggs -->
  - **IN-PROGRESS**: Heat frying pan on medium and melt butter <!-- id=heat_pan -->
  - **TODO**: Pour beaten eggs into the heated pan <!-- id=pour_eggs -->
  - **TODO**: Stir and scramble eggs with spatula until cooked <!-- id=scramble_eggs -->
  - **TODO**: Season with salt and pepper <!-- id=season -->
  - **TODO**: Serve scrambled eggs onto a plate <!-- id=serve -->
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTCD">
        Scrambled Eggs Simulation
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTBE</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.project</typename>
</object>

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

content.content:
You are a helpful assistant that can help with tasks in the outside world.
Be transparent about what you are doing and what you are not doing.
If you do not have tools to complete the task, inform the user.
DO NOT PRETEND TO DO SOMETHING YOU CAN'T DO.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTBE" name="Egg making">
    <spec>
      I'm testing how planning (task management) works.
  Create tasks to make scrambled eggs.
  
  Then simulate this plan execution in a markdown document.
  The document should reflect the state of all objects involved in the cooking process.
  The document should also have the log of actions taken.
  
  Important: simualte actions one by one, in the order they are listed.
  Simlate by updating the local document.
  
  <example>
    # State
  
    - 2 raw eggs
    - 1 frying pan
    
    # Action log
    
    - Taken 2 raw eggs out of the fridge.
  </example>
    </spec>
    <plan>
      - **DONE**: Gather ingredients: 2 eggs, butter, salt, pepper <!-- id=gather_ingredients -->
  - **DONE**: Gather tools: frying pan, spatula, bowl, fork <!-- id=gather_tools -->
  - **DONE**: Crack eggs into bowl and beat with fork <!-- id=crack_eggs -->
  - **DONE**: Heat frying pan on medium and melt butter <!-- id=heat_pan -->
  - **DONE**: Pour beaten eggs into the heated pan <!-- id=pour_eggs -->
  - **DONE**: Stir and scramble eggs with spatula until cooked <!-- id=scramble_eggs -->
  - **IN-PROGRESS**: Season with salt and pepper <!-- id=season -->
  - **TODO**: Serve scrambled eggs onto a plate <!-- id=serve -->
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTCD">
        Scrambled Eggs Simulation
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTBE</dxn>

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

content.content:
You are a helpful assistant that can help with tasks in the outside world.
Be transparent about what you are doing and what you are not doing.
If you do not have tools to complete the task, inform the user.
DO NOT PRETEND TO DO SOMETHING YOU CAN'T DO.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTBE" name="Egg making">
    <spec>
      I'm testing how planning (task management) works.
  Create tasks to make scrambled eggs.
  
  Then simulate this plan execution in a markdown document.
  The document should reflect the state of all objects involved in the cooking process.
  The document should also have the log of actions taken.
  
  Important: simualte actions one by one, in the order they are listed.
  Simlate by updating the local document.
  
  <example>
    # State
  
    - 2 raw eggs
    - 1 frying pan
    
    # Action log
    
    - Taken 2 raw eggs out of the fridge.
  </example>
    </spec>
    <plan>
      - **DONE**: Gather ingredients: 2 eggs, butter, salt, pepper <!-- id=gather_ingredients -->
  - **DONE**: Gather tools: frying pan, spatula, bowl, fork <!-- id=gather_tools -->
  - **DONE**: Crack eggs into bowl and beat with fork <!-- id=crack_eggs -->
  - **DONE**: Heat frying pan on medium and melt butter <!-- id=heat_pan -->
  - **DONE**: Pour beaten eggs into the heated pan <!-- id=pour_eggs -->
  - **DONE**: Stir and scramble eggs with spatula until cooked <!-- id=scramble_eggs -->
  - **DONE**: Season with salt and pepper <!-- id=season -->
  - **IN-PROGRESS**: Serve scrambled eggs onto a plate <!-- id=serve -->
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTCD">
        Scrambled Eggs Simulation
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTBE</dxn>

</object>

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

content.content:
You are a helpful assistant that can help with tasks in the outside world.
Be transparent about what you are doing and what you are not doing.
If you do not have tools to complete the task, inform the user.
DO NOT PRETEND TO DO SOMETHING YOU CAN'T DO.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTBE" name="Egg making">
    <spec>
      I'm testing how planning (task management) works.
  Create tasks to make scrambled eggs.
  
  Then simulate this plan execution in a markdown document.
  The document should reflect the state of all objects involved in the cooking process.
  The document should also have the log of actions taken.
  
  Important: simualte actions one by one, in the order they are listed.
  Simlate by updating the local document.
  
  <example>
    # State
  
    - 2 raw eggs
    - 1 frying pan
    
    # Action log
    
    - Taken 2 raw eggs out of the fridge.
  </example>
    </spec>
    <plan>
      - **DONE**: Gather ingredients: 2 eggs, butter, salt, pepper <!-- id=gather_ingredients -->
  - **DONE**: Gather tools: frying pan, spatula, bowl, fork <!-- id=gather_tools -->
  - **DONE**: Crack eggs into bowl and beat with fork <!-- id=crack_eggs -->
  - **DONE**: Heat frying pan on medium and melt butter <!-- id=heat_pan -->
  - **DONE**: Pour beaten eggs into the heated pan <!-- id=pour_eggs -->
  - **DONE**: Stir and scramble eggs with spatula until cooked <!-- id=scramble_eggs -->
  - **DONE**: Season with salt and pepper <!-- id=season -->
  - **DONE**: Serve scrambled eggs onto a plate <!-- id=serve -->
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTCD">
        Scrambled Eggs Simulation
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTA0" name="Expense tracking">
    <spec>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </spec>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTCB">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

</object>

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTA0" name="Expense tracking">
    <spec>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100

    </plan>

</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTA0" name="Expense tracking">
    <spec>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.

  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100

  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTA0" name="Expense tracking">
    <spec>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </spec>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTCB">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTA0" name="Expense tracking">
    <spec>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </spec>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTCB">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTA0" name="Expense tracking">
    <spec>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </spec>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTCB">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTA0" name="Expense tracking">
    <spec>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </spec>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTCB">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

</object>

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTA0" name="Expense tracking">
    <spec>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): ВЈ100
    </spec>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTCB">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>
